professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: We've been discussing how public libraries are reshaping themselves in mid-sized cities. Many branches now offer maker spaces, job-search workshops, and even free counseling sessions instead of focusing primarily on books. Some librarians argue this expansion keeps libraries essential to communities that no longer rely on them for reading materials, while others worry that the core mission — independent reading and research — gets crowded out when staff are stretched across too many programs. Should libraries lean further into community-service programming, or refocus on books and quiet study space?

students.name: Claire
students.text: Libraries should embrace community programming. Most households already have books and internet, so a branch that only lends materials feels increasingly redundant. By offering workshops and counseling, libraries become places where lower-income residents access services they couldn't otherwise afford, which fundamentally justifies the public funding.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I would refocus on reading and study space. A library cannot specialize in everything; when staff also run job-search programs and counseling sessions, the collection and reference help inevitably suffer. Cities should fund those services through dedicated agencies, not stretch one institution across unrelated missions.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: Bus rapid transit systems have recently expanded across Latin America and parts of the United States, with cities like Bogotá and Cleveland showing that dedicated lanes can move passengers nearly as quickly as a subway at a fraction of the cost. Some transportation planners argue that mid-sized cities should prioritize bus rapid transit over expensive rail expansion, while others believe rail remains the only sustainable backbone for growing metropolitan regions. Should mid-sized cities invest primarily in bus rapid transit, or hold out for rail?

students.name: Hailey
students.text: Bus rapid transit makes more sense for most mid-sized cities. The construction timeline for a subway easily stretches past a decade, while a dedicated BRT corridor can open in two or three years. That difference matters when residents need transit options now, not in 2040.

students.name: Eric
students.text: Rail is worth the wait. Once a metro line opens, it permanently anchors development around stations and shifts driving habits in ways that buses rarely do, since bus routes can be cut at any moment. The higher upfront investment buys decades of stability that buses fundamentally cannot match.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: Citizen assemblies — randomly selected residents asked to recommend policy on contested issues — have moved from political theory into real practice. Ireland used one to inform abortion legislation; France convened another on climate measures. Some political scientists argue these bodies cut through partisan deadlock by giving regular people time to study evidence together, while others insist that only elected legislatures, accountable at the ballot box, can legitimately set binding policy. Should national governments rely more on citizen assemblies for contentious decisions?

students.name: Paul
students.text: Citizen assemblies bring something legislatures fundamentally lack: months of structured deliberation by people who don't owe their next election to lobbyists or donors. On issues where lawmakers freeze because of polling pressure, an assembly can produce a coherent recommendation that elected officials then gain political cover to enact.

students.name: Zoe
students.text: Binding decisions belong with people the public can actually vote out. A randomly chosen panel may deliberate carefully, but if their recommendations later prove harmful, voters have no mechanism to remove them. Democratic accountability only works when those who decide also bear electoral consequences for the choice.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: We've been examining how schools handle heritage and indigenous languages in regions where the local community language differs from the national one. Several districts in New Mexico, Hawaii, and parts of Canada have begun requiring heritage-language coursework alongside the standard curriculum. Some educators argue these requirements preserve linguistic heritage that would otherwise disappear within a generation, while others worry that mandatory instruction crowds out time for subjects students need for college admissions. Should public schools require heritage-language coursework?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: Required coursework is the only realistic way to keep heritage languages alive. When instruction depends entirely on family transmission, fluency collapses once the older generation passes away. A mandatory program ensures every student in the community gets meaningful exposure, regardless of what their household speaks at home.

students.name: Andy
students.text: Schools already struggle to cover essential subjects within limited hours. Mandating heritage-language instruction sounds appealing, but it pushes other priorities — math, science, second-language courses students may actually use later — into the margins. Offering electives or after-school programs preserves the option without forcing that tradeoff.
